title: Wardle Academy pupils build skills and ambition during Aspire Day
excerpt: Pupils at Wardle Academy took part in first-aid training, careers activities, outdoor challenges, mock interviews and workshops during the school’s Aspire Day.
summary: Wardle Academy’s Aspire Day offered year groups a programme of activities designed to develop confidence, teamwork, resilience, career awareness and ambition.
content_html:
Learners across Wardle Academy have taken part in a special Aspire Day focused on developing confidence, teamwork, resilience and ambition.

Each year group followed a different programme, ranging from practical first-aid training and outdoor activities to careers workshops and mock interviews.

First aid, sport and creative workshops

Year 7 pupils took part in first-aid training, PSC sessions and a true-crime workshop delivered by Bonanza Creative.

They also joined the academy’s whole-school sports day, giving pupils an opportunity to work together and take part in physical activities.

Year 8 pupils visit the Peak District

Year 8 travelled to Castleton in the Peak District for a countryside walk with their form groups.

The academy said the walk allowed pupils to strengthen friendships, develop resilience and work together while enjoying the countryside and warm weather.

Planning for the future

Year 9 focused on a programme titled My Future, exploring possible careers, transferable skills and the qualities needed for future success.

Pupils also took part in energetic boot camp-style sessions on the school’s 3G pitch.

Year 10 followed a wider careers programme designed to prepare them for college and sixth-form applications in the autumn.

The programme included a presentation from JD Whys Up, a careers fair, personal-statement workshops and mock interviews with local employers.

Thanks to staff and local employers

Wardle Academy thanked the staff, visitors, employers and external providers who supported the day.

The school also praised learners for approaching the activities with enthusiasm and making the most of the opportunities available.

“Well done to our fantastic learners for embracing every opportunity with enthusiasm and positivity.”

This report is based on a public update published by Wardle Academy following its Aspire Day.

title: Rochdale marks national day of memory for victims of honour-based abuse
excerpt: Rochdale Borough Council has joined communities across the country in remembering victims of honour-based abuse and highlighting support available to people experiencing domestic violence.
summary: The National Day of Memory for Victims of Honour-Based Abuse is marked on 14 July, the birthday of Shafilea Ahmed, whose death became one of the UK’s most widely recognised cases of so-called honour-based killing.
content_html:
Rochdale Borough Council has joined communities across the country in marking the National Day of Memory for Victims of Honour-Based Abuse.

The annual day of remembrance takes place on 14 July and provides an opportunity to remember those whose lives have been lost through so-called honour-based abuse and to call for collective action to prevent further harm.

Remembering Shafilea Ahmed

The date was chosen because 14 July would have been the birthday of Shafilea Ahmed, a young woman whose life was taken after she was perceived to have brought shame upon her family.

Her case has become closely associated with national efforts to raise awareness of honour-based abuse, forced marriage and the risks faced by people subjected to family or community control.

Rochdale Borough Council said an estimated 12 lives are lost to so-called honour killings each year, although the true number is believed to be higher.

Support available in Rochdale

The council has reminded residents that its domestic abuse service supports people of any background or gender who are affected by domestic violence or abuse.

Support is also available for individuals who want help to change abusive behaviour.

Residents can contact the Rochdale domestic abuse service on 0161 507 9609 or visit the Rochdale Borough Council domestic violence and abuse support page.

Anyone who is in immediate danger should call 999.

Mental-health support

People who need urgent mental-health advice can call NHS 111 and select the mental-health option where available.

Information about local services is also available through the council’s mental-health support directory.

This report is based on a public statement issued by Rochdale Borough Council to mark the National Day of Memory for Victims of Honour-Based Abuse on 14 July 2026.

excerpt: There are renewed calls for legal changes to facilitate the deportation of a Rochdale rapist leader, highlighting ongoing concerns about justice and immigration policy.
summary: There are renewed calls for legal changes to facilitate the deportation of a Rochdale rapist leader, highlighting ongoing concerns about justice and immigration policy.
content_html:
ONGOING STORY — Updated 14 July 2026 at 20:30. 39 source updates have been combined into this article.

There are renewed calls for legal changes to facilitate the deportation of a Rochdale rapist leader, highlighting ongoing concerns about justice and immigration policy.

Latest update

Reform UK mayoral candidate Sian Astley has outlined a nine-point programme covering policing, transport, migration, housing, greenbelt protection and local government spending ahead of the Greater Manchester mayoral election.

Reform UK candidate Sian Astley has published a manifesto setting out her priorities ahead of the Greater Manchester mayoral election.

The document, which includes a message from Reform UK leader Nigel Farage, outlines a nine-point programme focused on policing, transport, local government spending, town-centre regeneration and housing.

Astley is standing against candidates from Labour and other parties in the race to become the next Mayor of Greater Manchester.

Earlier developments

The manifesto states that, if elected, Astley would seek to:

The manifesto argues that towns across Greater Manchester, including Rochdale, have not benefited equally from investment and development concentrated in Manchester city centre.

Astley says her campaign is focused on directing greater attention towards town centres, local services and community safety in boroughs outside the city centre.

The document also highlights concerns about housing, policing and local infrastructure as key issues facing residents.

One of the manifesto's most prominent pledges is a commitment to seek greater transparency regarding historic grooming-gang cases.

The manifesto states that Astley would seek the release of relevant records. Any disclosure of police, council or safeguarding information would remain subject to legal requirements, data-protection rules, victim safeguarding considerations and operational policing restrictions.

Astley also pledges to increase transparency around major development decisions and public spending within Greater Manchester.

The publication of the manifesto marks the latest stage of Reform UK's campaign in Greater Manchester.

title: Twelve Rochdale borough green spaces retain Green Flag Awards
excerpt: Twelve parks and green spaces across Heywood, Rochdale, Middleton and the Pennines have received Green Flag Awards for another year.
summary: Rochdale Borough Council has celebrated Green Flag Award success at 12 local green spaces, praising staff and volunteers who maintain the parks throughout the year.
content_html:
Twelve parks and green spaces across Rochdale borough are celebrating another year of Green Flag Award success.

The recognised sites are located across Heywood, Rochdale, Middleton and the Pennines.

Rochdale Borough Council described the awards as a significant achievement for the employees and volunteers who work throughout the year to keep the borough’s green spaces welcoming and accessible.

Recognition for well-managed green spaces

The Green Flag Award is an international quality mark for parks and green spaces.

Sites are assessed against standards covering areas such as cleanliness, environmental management, biodiversity, safety, community involvement and the quality of their facilities.

The awards recognise spaces that are well managed and provide benefits for residents, visitors and local wildlife.

Thanks to staff and volunteers

The council praised the teams and community volunteers who contribute to the upkeep of local parks throughout the seasons.

Their work includes maintaining paths, gardens and planted areas, caring for habitats and helping to ensure the spaces remain pleasant places for recreation and relaxation.

“A fantastic achievement for our teams and volunteers who work year-round to create welcoming spaces that offer so many benefits for residents and visitors to our borough.”

Residents are being encouraged to look out for Green Flags flying at their local award-winning park or green space.

This report is based on a public announcement and photographs published by Rochdale Borough Council following the latest Green Flag Award results.

title: Police appeal for footage after woman dies in M62 collision near Rochdale
excerpt: Police are appealing for dashcam and CCTV footage after a woman in her 50s died following a collision involving a Nissan Juke and BMW X5 on the M62 near Rochdale.
summary: Greater Manchester Police’s Serious Collision Investigation Unit is investigating a fatal collision between junctions 21 and 22 of the M62 and has asked witnesses or anyone with footage to come forward.
content_html:
Police are appealing for information after a woman died following a collision on the M62 near Rochdale.

Greater Manchester Police said officers were called at around 11pm on Saturday 11 July following reports of a collision involving a grey Nissan Juke and a grey BMW X5.

The incident happened between junctions 21 and 22 of the motorway.

Woman died at the scene

The driver of the Nissan, a woman aged in her 50s, suffered serious injuries in the collision.

Despite the efforts of emergency services, she was pronounced dead at the scene.

No further information about the woman has been released in the police appeal.

Dashcam and CCTV appeal

Greater Manchester Police’s Serious Collision Investigation Unit is investigating the circumstances surrounding the collision.

Officers want to hear from anyone who witnessed the incident or who may have dashcam or CCTV footage recorded in the area at around the time it happened.

Anyone who can assist should contact the investigation team on 0161 856 4741, quoting log 4349 of 11 July 2026.

Report What You Know

Information or footage for this investigation: Call Greater Manchester Police on 0161 856 4741 and quote log 4349 of 11/07/2026.

Report online: Information can also be submitted through the Greater Manchester Police reporting service.

Report anonymously: Contact Crimestoppers on 0800 555 111 or submit information anonymously online.

Emergency: Call 999 where there is an immediate danger to life or a serious incident is in progress.

This is an ongoing investigation. This report is based on a public appeal issued by Rochdale Police and Greater Manchester Police. Further verified updates will be added when they become available.
event_location: M62 between junctions 21 and 22, Rochdale

title: GTAS Vet Guard launches appeal to keep veterans’ support app free
excerpt: Volunteer-led organisation GTAS Vet Guard is asking for public support to help cover its growing operational costs while keeping its app free for veterans.
summary: GTAS Vet Guard has launched a donation appeal as it works to expand its services and maintain free access to its support app for veterans.
content_html:
GTAS Vet Guard has launched a public appeal to help keep its support app completely free for veterans as the volunteer-led organisation continues to grow.

The organisation provides support to veterans, their families and members of the wider community, with volunteers, partners and donors helping it reach more people across the UK.

GTAS Vet Guard said that its expansion has also brought increased running costs, including maintaining and improving its app, SMS messaging services, software subscriptions, safeguarding systems and other essential operational services.

From the organisation’s launch, it made a commitment that veterans would never have to pay to access the GTAS Vet Guard app.

The team said it does not want cost to become a barrier for anybody who may need support, companionship or practical help during a difficult period.

Donations will help the organisation maintain its existing services, develop new app features, improve the support it provides and reach more veterans and families.

“From day one, we made a promise that the GTAS Vet Guard app would always be free for veterans. That’s a promise we’re determined to keep.”

People who are able to support the appeal can make a contribution through the organisation’s online donation page: donate to GTAS Vet Guard here.

GTAS Vet Guard said donations of any size would help it continue providing a safe, reliable and accessible platform for people who have served in the Armed Forces.

Members of the public can also support the organisation by volunteering or sharing information about its work with veterans and their families.
image_alt: GTAS Vet Guard poster explaining how public support helps the organisation reach more veterans
image_caption: GTAS Vet Guard is seeking donations to help maintain and improve its free support services for veterans.

title: Bleakholt posts heartfelt tribute to Ann Widdecombe
excerpt: Bleakholt Animal Sanctuary has shared its sadness following the death of Ann Widdecombe, remembering her support for the charity and her visit to open its kitten unit.
summary: Bleakholt Animal Sanctuary has paid tribute to Ann Widdecombe, who visited the sanctuary in 2018 to open its £18,000 kitten unit.
content_html:
Bleakholt Animal Sanctuary has shared a heartfelt tribute to Ann Widdecombe following her death.

The sanctuary said everyone at Bleakholt was devastated by the news and remembered her fondly as a supporter of its work.

Ann visited Bleakholt in 2018, when she officially opened the charity’s new kitten unit, which had cost £18,000.

During her visit, she became particularly fond of a rabbit named Brandon.

Bleakholt said she remained in contact with the sanctuary following the visit and also made a donation to support its work caring for animals.

“She was a lovely lady who fell in love with a rabbit called Brandon while visiting us and kept in touch as well as making a donation.”

The sanctuary added that its thoughts were with Ann’s family and friends.

title: Rochdale residents warned over open-water dangers and moorland fires
excerpt: Rochdale Borough Council has urged residents to avoid entering open water and to keep barbecues, fires and fireworks away from the countryside during hot, dry weather.
summary: The council has issued summer safety warnings highlighting the dangers of open-water swimming and the risk that even a small flame could start a serious moorland fire.
content_html:
Residents across Rochdale borough are being urged to take extra care around open water and on the moors during periods of hot and dry weather.

Rochdale Borough Council has shared separate warnings about the risk of drowning and the danger of fires spreading rapidly through dry countryside.

Warning against entering open water

A water-safety campaign shared by the council warns people not to encourage friends to enter reservoirs, lakes, rivers or other open water.

“If you encourage your mates to go in the water it could be the last time you see them.”

Open water can contain hazards that are not visible from the surface, including sudden changes in depth, cold temperatures, currents and submerged objects.

The warning was produced with the Water Safety Partnership as part of efforts to prevent avoidable deaths and serious incidents.

No fires, barbecues or fireworks on moorland

The council has also reminded visitors that even a small flame can trigger a moorland fire during a prolonged dry spell.

People visiting the countryside are being told not to take disposable barbecues or camping stoves onto the moors and not to start open fires.

Visitors should also make sure cigarettes are fully extinguished and placed in a bin, while fireworks and sky lanterns should not be used in the countryside.

Council campaign material states that offences involving fires, barbecues or fireworks may result in a fine of up to £2,500 and, in some circumstances, a prison sentence.

What to do if a fire starts

Anyone who sees a fire should move away from the affected area and call 999 immediately.

People should not attempt to tackle a spreading moorland fire themselves, as conditions can change quickly and smoke can make it difficult to escape safely.

The council has asked residents and visitors to respect the countryside and help protect local wildlife, habitats and nearby communities.

This report is based on public safety material published by Rochdale Borough Council and the Water Safety Partnership.

title: Middleton Big Dig uncovers evidence of medieval Tonge Hall
excerpt: More than 350 people, including pupils from five local schools, took part in the Middleton Big Dig and uncovered evidence of activity dating from the medieval period.
summary: The community archaeological dig uncovered medieval ridge-and-furrow features and structures believed to relate to an earlier hall that stood on the Tonge Hall site before the existing Tudor building.
content_html:
Evidence of medieval farming and structures connected with an earlier version of Tonge Hall has been uncovered during a community archaeological dig in Middleton.

More than 350 people took part in the Middleton Big Dig, including pupils from five local schools.

Rochdale Borough Council said participants found evidence of activity spanning from the medieval period to the present day.

Medieval landscape discovered

Among the discoveries was evidence of ridge-and-furrow farming, a landscape feature created by historic ploughing methods widely used during the Middle Ages.

Archaeologists and volunteers also uncovered structures believed to relate to the original medieval hall that existed before the Tudor building now standing on the site.

The surviving building is known as Tonge Hall.

More than 350 people involved

The project involved residents, volunteers and children from five schools, giving young people the opportunity to take part in practical archaeology and learn more about Middleton’s history.

Pupils from St Peter’s RC Primary School were among those who visited the site and helped examine finds uncovered during the dig.

Photographs shared by the council show pupils working alongside members of the archaeological team and examining material recovered from the excavation area.

Finds to be cleaned and catalogued

The objects and other material discovered during the project will now be cleaned, recorded and catalogued.

Once that work has been completed, the finds are expected to be offered to Touchstones Rochdale for inclusion in its collection.

The dig formed part of the activity programme supporting the restoration of the Broadfield Park Slopes.

The wider project has received funding from the National Lottery Heritage Fund.

This report is based on a public update and photographs published by Rochdale Borough Council following the completion of the Middleton Big Dig.

title: Petition calls for defibrillators to be mandatory in all gyms
excerpt: A parliamentary petition is calling for every gym and exercise establishment to be legally required to have a defibrillator available.
summary: The petition, created by Eleanor Felton-Page, asks the Government to make automated external defibrillators mandatory in all gyms and exercise venues. It is due to close on 13 October 2026.
content_html:
A parliamentary petition is calling for defibrillators to become a legal requirement in every gym and exercise establishment.

The petition was created by Eleanor Felton-Page and is open for signatures until 13 October 2026.

It asks the Government to require gyms and other exercise venues to make an automated external defibrillator, commonly known as an AED, readily available in case somebody suffers a cardiac arrest.

Call for quicker access to lifesaving equipment

The petition argues that rapid access to a defibrillator could improve a person's chances of survival when cardiac arrest happens during or shortly after exercise.

Its supporting statement claims that more than 90% of sudden cardiac arrests involving young athletes occur during or immediately after exercise.

It also states that using an AED within three to five minutes could increase survival rates significantly. These figures are claims presented by the petition organiser and have not been independently verified by Rochdale Daily.

What is a defibrillator?

An automated external defibrillator is a portable device designed to analyse a person's heart rhythm and, where appropriate, deliver an electric shock to help restore a normal rhythm.

The devices provide spoken or visual instructions and can be used by members of the public while emergency help is on its way.

Anyone who believes a person is in cardiac arrest should call 999 immediately, begin CPR when instructed and send somebody to locate the nearest defibrillator where possible.

How to sign the petition

People wishing to support the proposal can sign the petition on the UK Parliament website.

Under the parliamentary petitions system, petitions that reach 10,000 signatures normally receive a Government response, while those reaching 100,000 signatures are considered for debate in Parliament.

This report is based on the wording of UK Parliament petition 764204. Medical and statistical claims attributed to the petition reflect the organiser's published case for the proposal.

title: Concerns Raised Over Abandoned Turner Brothers Factory in Rochdale
excerpt: Site owners warn of increased trespassing at the derelict Turner Brothers factory, known as Rochdale's Chernobyl, amid summer holidays and ongoing safety risks.
content_html:
ONGOING STORY — Updated 11 July 2026 at 15:33. 3 source updates have been combined into this article.

Site owners warn of increased trespassing at the derelict Turner Brothers factory, known as Rochdale's Chernobyl, amid summer holidays and ongoing safety risks.

Latest update

Site owners warn of increased trespassing at the derelict Turner Brothers factory, known as Rochdale's Chernobyl, amid summer holidays and ongoing safety risks.

A site in Rochdale known as the Turner Brothers factory has raised safety concerns among local residents. The factory, often referred to as Rochdale’s Chernobyl, has been surrounded by high fences for years, intended to deter trespassers.

However, these barriers have proven insufficient in protecting the site from outsiders. Reports indicate that the abandoned factory remains accessible, leading to fears about potential hazards associated with the derelict building.

Local authorities have been urged to take further action to secure the site and address the risks it poses to the community. The factory's deteriorating condition has sparked discussions about its future and the need for a comprehensive safety assessment.

Earlier developments

Anyone with information regarding the site or concerns about safety is encouraged to reach out to local authorities. Our thoughts are with the family and all those affected.

A site known as Rochdale's Chernobyl is facing increased trespassing issues as summer holidays approach. The Turner Brothers factory, once the world's largest asbestos manufacturer, is now abandoned and off-limits to the public. Site owners, Spodden Park Ltd, have reported a rise in trespassers, particularly during school holidays, with security alerted over 100 times in October 2025 alone.

Experts warn that asbestos fibres still present in the decaying buildings pose significant health risks to those who enter. Spodden Park Ltd has urged parents to remind their children of the dangers associated with the site, which includes the risk of asbestos exposure and potential injuries from collapsing structures. The company has produced a factsheet detailing the health hazards linked to asbestos, including asbestosis and lung cancer.

Last year, the number of trespassers increased by 25 per cent during school holidays, with estimates of 30 to 60 breaches per month. Locals have reported seeing children playing in the River Spodden, which flows through the factory site. Spodden Park Ltd has stated that entering the site requires breaking through a fence, which constitutes a crime, and has encouraged residents to report any such incidents to the police.

The site owners continue to monitor the area and work with security services to maintain safety. title: Geoff Hurst Reflects on His Iconic 1966 World Cup Hat-Trick
excerpt: As England prepares to face Norway, Geoff Hurst recalls his historic hat-trick in the 1966 World Cup final, marking 60 years since the memorable match.
content_html:
Geoff Hurst, the first player to score a hat-trick in a World Cup final, reflected on his iconic performance during England's 4-2 victory over West Germany in 1966. As England prepares to face Norway later today, Hurst shared his memories of that historic match, which took place at Wembley Stadium.

In the final minute of extra time, Hurst received a long ball from Bobby Moore while unmarked. Exhausted, he decided to strike the ball as hard as he could. "And of course, I mis-hit it, and it flew in," Hurst recalled in a 2018 interview with BBC Radio 4, reminiscing about the final goal of his hat-trick.

Born in 1941 in Ashton-under-Lyne, Hurst grew up in a footballing family. His father, Charlie Hurst, was also a professional footballer, having played for clubs including Oldham Athletic, Rochdale, and Mossley before moving south to join Chelmsford City.

As the anniversary of his remarkable achievement approaches, Hurst's contributions to English football remain celebrated, highlighting the significance of his performance in shaping the nation's sporting history.

excerpt: Landowners of the abandoned Turner Brothers factory site in Rochdale, known as 'Rochdale's Chernobyl', report a rise in trespassing incidents, particularly during school holidays, raising safety concerns.
content_html:
ONGOING STORY — Updated 11 July 2026 at 07:38. 3 source updates have been combined into this article.

Landowners of the abandoned Turner Brothers factory site in Rochdale, known as 'Rochdale's Chernobyl', report a rise in trespassing incidents, particularly during school holidays, raising safety concerns.

Latest update

A derelict factory site in Rochdale, referred to as 'Rochdale's Chernobyl', is experiencing rising concerns over trespassing incidents. The abandoned Turner Brothers factory, once the world's largest asbestos manufacturer, is located in a residential area just north of Rochdale town centre.

Landowners Spodden Park Ltd have reported a significant increase in trespassers, particularly during school holidays. In October 2025 alone, security was alerted to trespassing incidents more than 100 times, with estimates of 30 to 60 breaches per month, representing a 25 per cent rise during school holidays. Locals have also observed children playing in the River Spodden, which flows through the factory site.

The site remains off-limits to the public due to hazardous conditions, including the presence of asbestos fibres. Spodden Park Ltd has urged parents to educate their children about the dangers of entering the site, highlighting the risks of asbestos exposure and potential injuries from collapsing structures. They have produced a factsheet detailing the health risks associated with asbestos, including asbestosis and lung cancer.

As summer approaches, Spodden Park Ltd anticipates further increases in trespassing, particularly among local teenagers. They continue to monitor the site and work with security services to ensure safety, advising residents to report any illegal entry to the police.

title: Heywood HMO protest moves to Civic Centre ahead of 11 July demonstration
excerpt: Organisers have moved a protest over an HMO licence application beside Woodland Community Primary School from Heywood Memorial Gardens to Heywood Civic Centre.
summary: The demonstration is due to take place on Saturday 11 July, with Marlon West expected to speak. Campaigners are asking Rochdale Borough Council to refuse an HMO licence application for a property next to Woodland Community Primary School.
content_html:
A protest concerning an HMO licence application for a property next to Woodland Community Primary School is due to take place at Heywood Civic Centre on Saturday 11 July 2026.

Organisers originally planned to demonstrate at Heywood Memorial Gardens but agreed to move the event after objections to holding it at a place of remembrance.

Heywood HMO Protesters say the campaign concerns a specific application for a property next to the school. They are asking Rochdale Borough Council, as the local licensing authority, not to grant the HMO licence.

Marlon West is expected to speak at the demonstration.

Public exchange with Rochdale Council

The dispute became unusually public after Rochdale Borough Council used its official Facebook account to reply directly to the protest group beneath a post about the planned demonstration.

“Heywood HMO protesters councils are not responsible for asylum accommodation.”

The campaigners replied that their objection was not simply about which authority arranges asylum accommodation. Their argument is that Rochdale Borough Council still decides whether the property can legally operate as a licensed House in Multiple Occupation.

Although the demonstration has moved to the Civic Centre, the group has not withdrawn its objection to the application.

The council's licensing role

The Home Office is responsible for commissioning asylum-support accommodation and arranging placements through contracted providers. Rochdale Borough Council does not select individual occupants.

However, the council has a separate role as the HMO licensing authority. It must consider whether an application satisfies the relevant requirements, including the suitability of the property, its proposed occupancy, its management arrangements and whether the applicant and manager are fit and proper people.

If the legal licensing requirements are not met, the council can refuse the application or impose conditions.

The council's Facebook response addressed responsibility for asylum accommodation but did not directly answer the campaigners' point about the specific HMO licence application.

Limits on a refusal

The council cannot reject an application solely because of political pressure or the nationality, immigration status or asylum status of potential occupants.

Any decision must be based on lawful licensing and planning considerations. Campaigners may raise concerns about the building, management arrangements, proposed occupancy and its location next to a primary school, but the council must assess those concerns against the relevant legal tests.

Rochdale Daily has not independently confirmed the application reference, full property address or current stage of the licensing process.

An unusually direct intervention

It is unusual for a council's corporate social-media account to enter a public back-and-forth with a named protest group on a sensitive issue.

A fuller response could have explained the distinction between Home Office responsibility for arranging asylum accommodation and the council's responsibility for deciding HMO licence applications.

The short exchange risked creating confusion over which decisions are made by central government and which remain with the local authority.

The move to Heywood Civic Centre resolves the immediate disagreement over the protest venue, but attention will now return to the licence application itself.

Right of reply

Rochdale Borough Council is invited to confirm the application reference, property address, current status and the criteria being considered.

The applicant or property owner is invited to respond to the concerns raised by residents.

Heywood HMO Protesters are invited to provide the application documents and confirm the final start time of the demonstration.

This report is based on public Facebook statements and comments from Rochdale Borough Council and Heywood HMO Protesters, together with information supplied about the demonstration.

title: New Safe Haven offers support during nights out in Rochdale town centre
excerpt: A new Safe Haven has been introduced in Rochdale town centre, offering practical help and a place to pause for residents and visitors during nights out.
summary: The Rochdale Safe Haven provides phone charging, directions, water, first aid, emotional support and help getting home safely, with extended opening planned around major events and World Cup matches.
content_html:
A new Safe Haven has been introduced in Rochdale town centre to provide practical help and reassurance to residents and visitors enjoying the town’s nightlife.

The mobile facility is positioned in Town Hall Square, beside the Gracie Fields statue, and provides a recognised place for anyone who experiences difficulty or feels vulnerable during a night out.

Visitors can use the Safe Haven to charge their phones, ask for directions, get some water or take a moment away from the busy night-time environment.

Support available can also include basic first aid, emotional support, help reporting an incident, assistance arranging a taxi and signposting to other services.

Saturday-night support

Council plans state that the Safe Haven will ordinarily operate every Saturday evening between 10pm and 3am.

The facility can also act as a trusted central point for licensed venues, street pastors, Greater Manchester Police and other organisations working to keep people safe in the town centre.

Rochdale Borough Council said residents may see more of the Safe Haven during the summer as the town hosts major events.

Extended opening hours are also planned around World Cup matches, when more people may be visiting pubs, bars and venues across the town centre.

Initial six-month trial

Funding from the Greater Manchester Combined Authority was secured to establish the service as part of wider work to improve safety in Rochdale’s night-time economy.

The initial scheme is expected to run for six months while its cost, demand and impact are reviewed.

The council has said the service could later be used more widely at events and potentially extended to other town centres across the borough.

This report is based on information published by Rochdale Borough Council and an official council committee report.

title: Oulder Hill pupils discuss democracy during visit to Rochdale Town Hall
excerpt: Pupils from Oulder Hill Leadership Academy met the Mayor and Mayoress of Rochdale during an educational visit to the Mayor’s Parlour and council chamber.
summary: Mayor Councillor Faisal Rana and Mayoress Iram Faisal welcomed Oulder Hill pupils to Rochdale Town Hall, where students asked questions about civic leadership, local democracy and how the council serves the community.
content_html:
Pupils from Oulder Hill Leadership Academy have visited Rochdale Town Hall to learn more about civic leadership, local democracy and the work of the borough council.

The students were welcomed to the Mayor’s Parlour and council chamber by the Mayor of Rochdale, Councillor Faisal Rana, and Mayoress Iram Faisal.

During the visit, pupils were given an opportunity to see some of the town hall’s civic rooms and sit inside the chamber where councillors debate and make decisions affecting communities across the borough.

The students asked questions about the role of the mayor, how local democracy works and how Rochdale Borough Council serves local residents.

They also spoke about their own ambitions and aspirations for the future.

Councillor Rana described it as an “absolute pleasure” to welcome the pupils and praised their confidence, curiosity and enthusiasm.

A personal connection to Oulder Hill

The mayor said the visit held particular significance for him because his own children attended Oulder Hill, with one having served as the school’s head boy.

He said seeing a new generation of pupils sitting in the chamber where important decisions are made for the borough was a special moment.

Councillor Rana congratulated the pupils for representing their school and thanked staff for helping to make the visit a success.

He said the young people should be proud of themselves and expressed his hope that they would go on to achieve their ambitions in the years ahead.

Learning about local government

Educational visits to civic buildings can give pupils a closer look at how local decisions are made and how elected representatives, council officers and ceremonial office holders serve their communities.

The visit also allowed the Oulder Hill pupils to raise their own questions directly with the borough’s mayor and mayoress.

This report is based on a public social-media statement and photographs shared by Mayor of Rochdale Councillor Faisal Rana. No pupils have been identified by name.

title: Reform mayoral candidate Sian Astley stands by controversial migrant comments
excerpt: Reform UK’s Greater Manchester mayoral candidate Sian Astley has defended comments suggesting Green voters and politicians should house people she described as illegal criminals, rapists and economic chancers.
summary: Sian Astley said her comments were partly tongue-in-cheek and directed at supporters of open-border policies, while Labour accused Reform UK of seeking to punish communities according to how they vote.
content_html:
Reform UK’s candidate for Mayor of Greater Manchester has said she stands by controversial comments suggesting supporters of other political parties should take responsibility for housing people she described as illegal criminals, rapists and economic chancers.

Sian Astley made the comments while sharing a Reform UK proposal concerning the location of migrant detention centres.

Under the proposal, facilities would be prioritised in areas represented or controlled by Green Party politicians, while areas with Reform UK-controlled councils or MPs would not receive them.

Astley suggested that Green voters, politicians from what she called the “uniparty” and councillors should instead house the people concerned themselves.

Speaking to the Manchester Evening News after the comments attracted criticism, Astley said she stood by what she had written.

She described the suggestion as partly “tongue in cheek” and argued that people who advocate open-border policies should accept the consequences of those policies.

Labour criticism

Labour Party chair Anna Turley criticised both the comments and the wider proposal.

She accused Reform UK of supporting a policy that would reward or punish communities according to how residents voted and said this undermined basic democratic principles.

Astley has not withdrawn the comments and said criticism should instead be directed at the immigration system and those entering the country unlawfully.

Greater Manchester mayoral election

Astley is Reform UK’s official candidate in the Greater Manchester mayoral election taking place on Thursday 30 July 2026.

title: Whitworth councillor challenges council over play park funding gap
excerpt: Whitworth councillor Scott Smith has challenged Rossendale Borough Council over play park funding, claiming the town is receiving no comparable investment while substantial sums are being spent elsewhere.
summary: Rossendale opposition leader Scott Smith says council officers will meet Whitworth Town Council within two months after he questioned what he described as unequal play park investment.
content_html:
A Whitworth councillor has questioned why the town is not receiving what he considers the same level of play park investment as other parts of Rossendale.

Councillor Scott Smith, leader of the opposition at Rossendale Borough Council, raised the issue during question time connected to the full council meeting held on Wednesday 8 July.

“What on Earth will it take for Whitworth to receive equal treatment to every other town in this borough?”

In a social-media statement, Councillor Smith claimed that £140,000 is being spent on Edgeside Park and a further £143,000 on Whitaker Park in Rawtenstall during 2026, while Whitworth is currently set to receive no comparable play park investment.

He said a cabinet member committed council officers to meeting representatives of Whitworth Town Council within two months to put together a plan aimed at changing the position.

Councillor Smith said he intended to hold the council administration to that commitment.

Edgeside investment confirmed

Rossendale Borough Council announced on 7 July that it was moving ahead with the next phase of improvements to the top play area at Edgeside Park.

The council said the project was being supported through Pride in Place funding, with additional investment secured through the Derbyshire Environmental Fund via the Landfill Communities Fund.

However, the council's announcement did not state the £140,000 figure cited by Councillor Smith. Rochdale Daily has also not independently confirmed the £143,000 Whitaker Park figure or the claim that no comparable play park funding has been allocated to Whitworth.

Right of reply

Rossendale Borough Council is welcome to provide clarification about the funding figures and the proposed meeting with Whitworth Town Council.

Source
legal_disclaimer: The spending figures and account of the proposed meeting are attributed to Councillor Scott Smith. Rossendale Borough Council's published Edgeside Park announcement confirms investment but does not state the figures cited.

title: Trial begins for man accused of murdering teenager in Middleton
excerpt: Terrence King is on trial for the alleged murder of 15-year-old Devon Simmonds-Caines, who was struck by King's car after stealing an e-bike.
content_html:
A trial has commenced at Manchester's Minshull Street Crown Court for Terrence King, who faces allegations of murdering 15-year-old Devon Simmonds-Caines in Middleton last year. The incident occurred on 22 July 2025, when Simmonds-Caines was struck by King's vehicle after allegedly stealing an e-bike from another boy.

The prosecution, led by Michael Hayton KC, contends that King, 60, deliberately drove his car at Simmonds-Caines as an act of revenge. CCTV and doorbell camera footage presented in court reportedly shows King accelerating before veering onto the pavement and colliding with the teenager, who succumbed to his injuries at the scene.

King has admitted to manslaughter, acknowledging that his actions resulted in Simmonds-Caines's death, but he denies the charge of murder. The prosecution argues that this incident was not merely a tragic accident or a case of poor driving, but rather a calculated act of aggression against a boy who had stolen the e-bike.

The footage shown in court was described as distressing, prompting some family members of Simmonds-Caines to leave the courtroom in tears. This case has attracted significant attention, particularly due to the victim's age and the circumstances surrounding his death, raising broader concerns about youth crime and vigilantism.

The community in Middleton is now grappling with the implications of such incidents, which raise pressing questions about safety and the appropriate responses to crime. The trial is expected to continue over the coming weeks, with further evidence and testimonies to be presented. Local residents, particularly those affected by the incident, may be closely following the proceedings as they seek to comprehend the full context of this tragic event and its impact on community safety.

Rochdale is gearing up for a warm and sunny week, with the Met Office forecasting maximum temperatures reaching 31°C on Friday, July 10. The pleasant weather is expected to continue throughout the weekend, making it an ideal time for outdoor activities and events in the area.

As the week progresses, the heat will intensify, with Friday marking the hottest day, where it will feel like 32°C due to humidity. Saturday will see a slight dip in temperatures, with a maximum of 28°C, but it will remain sunny. Sunday will continue the trend with highs of 26°C, providing a perfect opportunity for families to enjoy local parks and recreational areas. The forecast indicates minimal chances of rain throughout the week, with less than 10% expected on most days.

As the week unfolds, temperatures will remain steady, with Monday and Tuesday both expected to reach 26°C. The evenings will cool down to around 14-17°C, making for comfortable nights. Residents are encouraged to stay hydrated and protect themselves from the sun during peak hours, especially on the hotter days.

This warm weather is significant for local businesses, particularly those in the hospitality and outdoor leisure sectors, as it may boost foot traffic and sales. Parks and outdoor venues are likely to see increased visitors, which can positively impact the local economy.

For those planning outdoor activities, it is advisable to check the weather regularly and prepare for high pollen counts. Emergency services, including police, firefighters and paramedics, were called to the scene at around 12.30pm. Upon arrival, they discovered the body of a woman inside the stables. The victim has since been named as Laura Foulis, aged in her 30s.

Following the incident, a man in his 40s was taken into custody by Greater Manchester Police. He has since been bailed pending further investigation. A spokesperson for Rochdale Coroners' Court confirmed that a referral has been made regarding Ms Foulis' death, and an inquest will be opened at a later date.

Witnesses reported seeing large flames and thick black smoke rising from the stables, prompting a swift response from emergency services. Forensic investigators remained at the scene for several days following the fire, which has since been cordoned off as part of the ongoing investigation.

Tributes have been shared on social media, with friends and family remembering Ms Foulis as a kind and beautiful person who inspired many with her love of horses. One tribute described her as a 'kind soul' who made a significant impact on those around her.

title: Temporary fire station in Heywood approved during redevelopment
excerpt: Greater Manchester Fire and Rescue Service has secured approval for a temporary fire station in Heywood, ensuring continuous emergency services while the existing facility undergoes significant redevelopment.
content_html:
ONGOING STORY — This report brings together confirmed developments from multiple sources.

Greater Manchester Fire and Rescue Service (GMFRS) has received approval to establish a temporary fire station in Heywood, facilitating the redevelopment of the existing facility. The new structure will be situated behind the current station on Middleton Road, ensuring that emergency response services remain operational throughout the rebuilding process.

The existing fire station, which has served the Heywood community for nearly 60 years, is scheduled for demolition as part of this redevelopment initiative. The temporary station will consist of a two-storey modular building, equipped with a bay for one fire engine, while an additional outbuilding will house any specialist vehicles. This arrangement is intended to guarantee that emergency services continue without interruption during the construction period.

Planning documents reveal that the temporary fire station is strategically located just 400 metres from Junction 19 of the M62 motorway, positioning it well for rapid emergency response. GMFRS has stated that the new facility will be a modern, fit-for-purpose community fire station, designed to meet both current and future operational needs. It will also enhance facilities for staff training and community engagement, reflecting the service's commitment to adapting to the evolving demands of the community.

This redevelopment is part of a wider £38 million Estates Strategy by GMFRS, aimed at upgrading and modernising several fire stations across the Greater Manchester area. The strategy underscores the importance of ensuring that fire services can provide optimal support to residents, adapting to changing community needs and operational requirements. A separate planning application for the demolition of the existing station and the construction of the new community fire station is expected to be submitted shortly.

The anticipated new facility will include a training tower, vehicle parking, and a dedicated training yard, which will further enhance GMFRS's capabilities in the region. For local families, businesses, and schools, the uninterrupted provision of emergency services during the redevelopment is crucial, as it ensures that safety and response times remain unaffected. Local residents and stakeholders are encouraged to monitor the progress of these plans, as the redevelopment will play a vital role in maintaining safety and emergency response in Heywood. Updates will be disseminated through GMFRS and local news outlets as the project advances.

title: Health experts issue guidance on safe fan use amid Rochdale heatwave
excerpt: As temperatures rise, health experts in Rochdale provide essential advice on the safe operation of electric fans to mitigate overheating and electrical hazards.
content_html:
ONGOING STORY — This report brings together confirmed developments from multiple sources.

As the UK endures a significant heatwave, health and safety experts are advising Rochdale residents on the safe operation of electric fans. This guidance, issued by High Speed Training, underscores the importance of taking precautions to prevent overheating and potential electrical hazards as households increasingly rely on fans for relief from the soaring temperatures.

Dr Richard Anderson, Director of Learning at High Speed Training, emphasised the effectiveness of fans in providing comfort during hot weather while also stressing the necessity of their proper use to ensure safety. He noted that as temperatures rise, many individuals will be reaching for their fans, but adherence to safety protocols is crucial to avoid accidents.

Residents should register new fans with their manufacturers to receive important updates regarding safety recalls. Before using a fan, it is vital to inspect it for any signs of damage, particularly focusing on the plug and cable. Fans that produce buzzing or crackling noises, or exhibit warm plugs, should be turned off immediately and examined by a qualified electrician. Regular cleaning is also important; dust accumulation on fan blades can strain the motor and increase the risk of overheating. Users must always unplug the fan before cleaning and follow the manufacturer's reassembly instructions.

Furthermore, it is advisable to avoid leaving fans running unattended or overnight while sleeping. Instead, residents might consider cooling their bedrooms prior to bedtime by operating the fan with windows open when appropriate. Proper positioning of fans is equally important; they should be placed on a flat, stable surface and kept clear of curtains or other materials that could obstruct airflow or create hazards.

Dr Anderson reiterated the significance of these safety measures, asserting that dedicating a few moments to simple safety checks can prevent fires or accidents and ensure fans operate smoothly. With the heatwave projected to persist for at least another week, adhering to these safety tips will be crucial for maintaining comfort and safety during this period. For further information or to report safety concerns, residents can contact local health and safety authorities.

title: Proposal for new SEND school at Kingsway West Business Park
excerpt: Whitehall Academy has submitted plans to convert a vacant warehouse into a school for children with special educational needs and disabilities, accommodating up to 50 pupils.
content_html:
ONGOING STORY — This report brings together confirmed developments from multiple sources.

A proposal has been submitted to transform a vacant warehouse at Kingsway West Business Park in Rochdale into a new educational facility for children with special educational needs and disabilities (SEND). The plans, put forward by Whitehall Academy, aim to create a school that will accommodate up to 50 pupils, providing essential support for children with complex needs.

The design of the proposed school includes six classrooms, three sensory rooms, and a multi-purpose hall, alongside various support spaces such as offices and a medical room. While the exterior of the building will remain unchanged, a secure play area featuring artificial grass is planned for the west side, utilising previously unused space. This initiative represents an extension of Whitehall Academy's existing operations on Whitehall Street, where the independent primary school already focuses on supporting vulnerable children with diverse needs.

Students at the proposed facility may include those diagnosed with autism and learning difficulties, all of whom will have an Education, Health, and Care Plan (EHCP) or be undergoing the assessment process. The school is expected to operate from 9am to 3pm for pupils, with staff hours extending from 8am to 4pm. There are also plans for breakfast and after-school clubs, which could extend the school day until 5pm, providing additional support for families.

The warehouse, previously occupied by Circuitmaster Designs Ltd, an engineering firm, has been vacant since October 2024. The conversion of this space into a SEND school is likely to address a pressing need for specialised educational facilities in the area, particularly as the demand for such services continues to grow.

The establishment of a SEND school at Kingsway West Business Park could have significant implications for local families and the wider community. Parents of children with special educational needs may find increased access to tailored educational resources and support, reducing the need for travel to distant facilities. Additionally, the school could foster a more inclusive environment within the neighbourhood, promoting awareness and understanding of SEND issues. As the proposal progresses, local stakeholders, including parents and community organisations, may wish to engage with the planning process to ensure that the needs of all affected parties are considered.

title: Thomas O’Rourke sentenced to 26 years for decades of child abuse
excerpt: Thomas O’Rourke has been sentenced to 26 years in prison after being convicted of multiple sexual offences against five children over a span of more than twenty years. The case highlights the importance of reporting abuse, regardless of when it occurred.
content_html:
Thomas O’Rourke, a 74-year-old resident of Rochdale, has been sentenced to 26 years in prison following his conviction for 31 sexual offences against five children. The sentencing took place at Manchester Crown Court on 6 July 2026, after a trial that revealed the extensive nature of his crimes, which occurred between the 1970s and 1990s in areas including Middleton and Blackley.

The charges against O’Rourke included serious offences such as rape, sexual assault, and indecent assault. During the trial, survivors delivered poignant victim impact statements, articulating the enduring effects of the abuse they suffered. One survivor spoke of her resolve to reclaim her voice and confront the pain inflicted by O’Rourke's actions. Another survivor expressed her struggle with the label of victim, hoping that O’Rourke would come to feel remorse for the suffering he caused. A third survivor reflected on the disruption to her childhood and teenage years, acknowledging that while it took time for her to report the abuse, her journey towards recovery would be even more protracted.

The investigation into O’Rourke’s actions commenced in 2019 when the first survivor bravely came forward, prompting detectives to uncover additional victims. Detective Inspector Wilcock, who led the investigation, praised the survivors for their courage and commitment to seeking justice, underscoring the police's determination to pursue cases of abuse irrespective of when they occurred. O’Rourke was characterised as a predator who exploited the trust of children, and his sentence serves as a powerful reminder that survivors can seek justice at any point in their lives.

This case has significant implications for the local community, particularly for families and educational institutions. The revelations surrounding O’Rourke's actions may encourage other survivors to come forward, fostering a culture of openness and support. Schools and community organisations may need to enhance their safeguarding measures and provide additional resources to support children and families affected by abuse. The case also highlights the importance of ongoing education about consent and healthy relationships, which could help prevent future incidents.

As the community processes the impact of this case, it is crucial for local support services to remain proactive and accessible. The police and various organisations are likely to continue their outreach efforts to ensure that survivors feel empowered to report abuse and receive the necessary support. The message from this case is clear: justice can be pursued at any time, and there is a network of support available for those who have suffered.

title: Heywood Residents Report Drainage Issues and Unpleasant Smell
excerpt: Residents of a Heywood apartment block have faced unsanitary conditions due to drainage issues linked to improper waste disposal, prompting calls for better management from their housing association.
content_html:
Residents of a newly developed apartment block on Rochdale Road East in Heywood have been grappling with persistent foul odours and unsanitary conditions stemming from drainage issues that have persisted for several days. The problems, first reported on Friday, July 3, have been linked to residents flushing inappropriate items, such as nappies and baby wipes, down the toilets, resulting in significant blockages within the drainage system.

The situation escalated dramatically when residents began to notice leaks in the underground car park and communal bin shed, with reports of faeces on the floors and a stench described as reminiscent of 'rotten eggs'. One resident, who preferred to remain anonymous, recounted the distressing conditions, stating that the overpowering smell had caused nausea among several residents, including a pregnant woman. Despite numerous calls to First Choice Homes Oldham, the housing association responsible for the building, residents felt their concerns were not being adequately addressed, expressing frustration at what they perceived as dismissive responses from staff.

"We feel like we are being ignored," said one resident, reflecting the sentiments of many in the building, which accommodates 40 apartments. In response to the complaints, First Choice Homes Oldham acknowledged the issue, confirming that the blockage was cleared on Monday, July 7, after being assessed over the preceding weekend. A spokesperson for the housing association indicated that the problem had been exacerbated by improper waste disposal practices among residents, and they intend to remind tenants of the importance of not flushing inappropriate items down the toilets.

The residents' grievances have raised broader concerns regarding the management of the property, particularly as the building remains within its defects period. While the housing association confirmed that necessary repairs were conducted by contractors, residents are left questioning the effectiveness of the response and the ongoing management of their living conditions. This incident underscores the critical importance of proper waste disposal and the potential ramifications of neglecting such practices.

As the area prepares for a second heatwave, residents are increasingly anxious about the implications of unsanitary conditions in their living environment. The combination of heat and poor hygiene could exacerbate health risks, particularly for vulnerable individuals. Many residents have expressed a desire for more proactive communication and support from First Choice Homes to prevent similar issues from arising in the future. The situation serves as a reminder of the need for effective property management and community cooperation to ensure a safe and healthy living environment.

This programme is part of a wider strategy across Greater Manchester to promote healthier lifestyles, improve air quality, and alleviate traffic congestion within the borough. Delivered by Your Trust Rochdale in collaboration with Rochdale Borough Council, Transport for Greater Manchester, and Endurance Hub, the initiative seeks to make active travel the preferred choice for local trips, potentially reducing the number of car journeys made in the area.

The initiative's objectives highlight that millions of journeys in Greater Manchester are currently undertaken by car, many of which could be easily completed on foot or by bicycle. By increasing the number of residents who choose active travel options, the programme aims to enhance community health and wellbeing while contributing to environmental sustainability. Various activities and resources will be made available throughout the borough to support walking, wheeling, and cycling, fostering a culture of active travel within the community.

The focus of the initiative extends beyond individual health benefits; it also aims to create a more vibrant and accessible environment for all residents. By promoting active travel, the initiative could lead to a reduction in traffic congestion, which may improve journey times for those who still rely on motor vehicles. Families, particularly those with children, may find it easier to navigate local streets safely, while businesses could benefit from increased foot traffic as more residents choose to walk or cycle to local shops and services.

For those interested in learning more about the Active Travel initiative or participating in upcoming activities, the Active Travel team can be contacted via email at activetravel@yourtrustrochdale.co.uk or by calling 01706 922437. This outreach aims to engage the community and gather feedback on how to improve active travel options in Rochdale, ensuring that the initiative meets the needs of local residents.

The launch of this initiative is particularly relevant for Rochdale residents as it aligns with ongoing efforts to promote sustainable transport solutions and enhance the quality of life in the borough. As the programme develops, residents can expect to see an increase in resources and support for active travel, making it easier and more appealing to choose walking or cycling over driving. This shift could lead to a healthier, more connected community, with long-term benefits for both individual wellbeing and the local environment.

title: Driver crashes into wall and parked van in Littleborough
excerpt: Two people were injured in a crash on Halifax Road in Littleborough, near a local pub, on July 4. Emergency services attended the scene shortly after the incident.
content_html:
A driver crashed into a wall and collided with a parked van on Halifax Road in Littleborough on July 4, resulting in injuries to two people. Emergency services were called to the scene at around 10.15pm following reports of the incident outside The Moorcock pub, near Blackstone Edge.

According to Greater Manchester Police, a car had come off Halifax Road just before the collision occurred. The two occupants of the vehicle managed to exit before emergency services arrived. One man was taken to hospital for treatment, while the injuries of both occupants are not believed to be life-threatening or life-changing.

Firefighters from Littleborough and Rochdale fire stations, along with a technical rescue unit from Ashton fire station, responded to the crash. A spokesperson for Greater Manchester Fire and Rescue Service confirmed that the two occupants self-extricated from the vehicle and were assessed at the scene by paramedics from the North West Ambulance Service before being taken to hospital.

No arrests have been made in connection with the incident. Greater Manchester Police stated that officers responded to reports of a collision involving several vehicles, with one vehicle colliding with parked cars. The scene was made safe before being handed over to police around half an hour later.

title: Metrolink services in Rochdale disrupted by £150m upgrade works
excerpt: Three Metrolink lines will be closed for three weeks from 13 July as part of a £150 million upgrade, impacting local transport and commuter journeys.
content_html:
Transport for Greater Manchester (TfGM) has announced that three Metrolink lines will be closed for three weeks, commencing 13 July, due to essential upgrade works. This initiative is part of a broader £150 million investment aimed at enhancing the tram network across Greater Manchester. The lines affected by this closure include the Eccles, Trafford Centre, and Altrincham routes, which will not operate during this period. Furthermore, the East Didsbury and Manchester Airport lines will terminate at Firswood, while services on the Rochdale line will conclude at Exchange Square. The disruption is anticipated to last until 2 August.

Ian Davies, the network director for Metrolink, emphasised that the work is being carried out on one of the busiest stretches of tram track in the country, specifically between Trafford Bar, Cornbrook, and Deansgate-Castlefield. In addition to the tram line closures, further works will take place at Eccles and around Piccadilly Gardens in the city centre. This comprehensive approach is intended to minimise future disruptions to the network, ultimately ensuring a more reliable service once the upgrades are completed.

The impact of these closures is likely to be felt by commuters, families, and local businesses. Those relying on the affected tram services may need to adjust their travel plans, potentially leading to longer journey times as they switch to replacement bus services. TfGM has stated that these bus services will be available between the affected stops, but passengers should anticipate delays and plan accordingly. The timing of the upgrade works coincides with the special event bus service for The Hundred cricket matches at Old Trafford on 26 and 31 July, which could further complicate travel arrangements for those attending the matches or commuting in the area.

TfGM encourages passengers to remain informed about service changes and to check their travel options in advance. Updates will be provided through TfGM's official channels, ensuring that commuters have access to the latest information regarding tram services and replacement bus routes. This proactive communication is essential for minimising inconvenience during the upgrade period, as many local residents and commuters will be affected by the changes.

As the upgrade progresses, the long-term benefits of improved tram services are expected to enhance connectivity and reliability for the Rochdale area. While the immediate disruptions may pose challenges, the investment in infrastructure is likely to yield a more efficient transport network in the future, ultimately benefiting the local community.

title: Children enjoy Tin Man and the Scarecrow show at Smallbridge Library
excerpt: Families gathered at Smallbridge Library for a performance of Tin Man and the Scarecrow featuring storytelling, puppetry, choreography and original music.
summary: Children and families attended a special performance of Tin Man and the Scarecrow at Smallbridge Library, supported by arts and cultural organisations.
content_html:
Children and families enjoyed an afternoon of storytelling, puppetry and music during a special performance at Smallbridge Library.

Tin Man and the Scarecrow was presented to an audience of local families as part of a programme of cultural activities taking place across Rochdale borough libraries.

Photographs shared after the event showed children meeting the performers and taking part in activities inside the library.

Storytelling, puppetry and music

The production combined storytelling and puppetry with bold choreography and original music composed by award-winning composer Oleta Haffner.

Rochdale Borough Libraries thanked everyone who attended the Saturday performance and helped make the event a success.

The show was designed to introduce children to live performance in an accessible and familiar community setting.

Supported by cultural organisations

The performance was supported by Arts Council England, the Granada Foundation, Rochdale Borough Culture Network, Dance at Salford and Bradford Arts Centre.

Smallbridge Library hosts activities for children and families alongside its regular library services.

This report is based on a public Facebook update and photographs published by Rochdale Borough Libraries following the performance at Smallbridge Library.

title: Police Officer Rushed to Hospital After Middleton Crash
excerpt: A police officer was taken to hospital after a crash involving a Greater Manchester Police van in Middleton. The officer's injuries are not believed to be serious.
content_html:
A police officer was taken to hospital following a crash involving a Greater Manchester Police van in Middleton. The incident occurred on Townley Street at approximately 1.10pm on Friday, July 3.

The officer was transported to hospital for assessment and treatment. According to Greater Manchester Police (GMP), his injuries are not believed to be life-threatening or life-changing. No other injuries were reported as a result of the crash.

No arrests have been made in connection with the incident. This crash was one of two similar incidents involving police vans on the same day in Greater Manchester.

Earlier that day, a British Transport Police van was involved in a collision with a blue Kia Picanto on Rochdale Road in Harpurhey at around 8.45pm. At the time of that incident, officers were transporting a 28-year-old man to police custody.

GMP has not provided further details regarding the circumstances of the Middleton crash. Investigations are ongoing, and the force is likely to review the incident as part of standard procedure. The police van was responding to an incident at the time of the crash, but specific details have not yet been disclosed.

Anyone with information can find Greater Manchester Police and Crimestoppers contact details at the end of this article. Our thoughts are with the family and all those affected.

title: Edgar Wood Academy Celebrates First GCSE Cohort Following Ofsted Praise
excerpt: Edgar Wood Academy marks a significant milestone as its first Year 11 students complete their GCSEs, coinciding with a positive Ofsted report highlighting the school's transformation.
content_html:
Edgar Wood Academy in Middleton is celebrating a major milestone as its first Year 11 students have completed their GCSEs. This achievement comes shortly after the school received a highly positive report from Ofsted, which praised its transformation since opening in September 2021.

The academy began with a single Year 7 cohort and has since welcomed new year groups each September. Inspectors who visited the school in June 2026 commended the calm and purposeful environment, noting that leaders' efforts to establish an orderly setting have been effective. Classrooms were described as typically calm places conducive to learning, with students feeling safe and supported.

Year 11 student Harriett expressed her positive experience at the academy, stating, "I would describe my time at Edgar Wood as really positive and welcoming." Another student, Josiah, highlighted the support from teachers, saying they have taken time to assist him with studying and revision.

Headteacher Paul Jones expressed pride in the school's progress, stating, "We are absolutely delighted with this report. It is a recognition of the hard work, dedication and ambition shown every day by our staff, students and families." He emphasised the significance of the first cohort's journey alongside the school, noting their role in shaping its culture.

title: Kirkholt Pantry Celebrates 10 Years of Supporting Local Families
excerpt: Kirkholt Pantry has marked its 10th anniversary with a celebration, recognising its role in providing affordable food and support to local residents.
content_html:
Kirkholt Pantry has celebrated its 10th anniversary at The Strand, acknowledging a decade of service to the Kirkholt community. The pantry has distributed over 23,000 bags of essential food items since its inception, helping households manage rising living costs.

Colleagues, customers and volunteers gathered this week to commemorate the milestone, including some of the original founders of the pantry. The initiative allows members to collect a bag of essential food items for £4 each week and operates every Thursday from 10am until 12pm.

Rochdale Boroughwide Housing reported that the pantry has played a crucial role in supporting local families and expressed gratitude to all who have contributed to its success. Councillor Daniel Meredith, who helped establish the pantry, described it as a lifeline for residents, providing affordable access to essential food and alleviating pressure on household budgets.

Councillor Meredith thanked volunteers and donors on Facebook for their ongoing support over the past decade, highlighting the pantry as a shining example of community spirit and partnership in action.
